title: Data Quality Portal
text: 🔢 Overall: 65 grouped issues (18 High priority)

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_composition
Flagged observations: 1712 GX Assertions: 4 Passed, 1 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the mv_biomass_composition dataset, comprising 6,366 records, reveals significant data quality concerns primarily driven by extreme statistical skew and physically impossible values. The dataset exhibits a massive range in the ‘observed_value’ column, spanning from -244.0 to 161,000.0, with a mean (1,514.54) that is vastly higher than the median (33.15). This suggests a critical lack of unit standardization, likely mixing percentage-based compositional data (0-100%) with parts-per-million (mg/kg) or parts-per-billion measurements without proper normalization.
A notable portion of the anomalies (1,712 flagged records) is clustered around specific agricultural residues—namely almond hulls, shells, and grape/tomato pomace. While ‘analyst_name’ is entirely unpopulated, ‘analyst_email’ provides sufficient traceability for approximately 80% of the records. Key providers such as ‘tiny’, ‘humorous’, and ‘capstan’ show consistent patterns of low-severity deviations in structural carbohydrate and lignin measurements, suggesting minor but systematic differences in laboratory hydrolysis protocols or baseline calibration across different research facilities. Immediate attention is required for negative values and high-magnitude outliers that threaten the integrity of downstream biomass conversion models.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Physically Impossible Negative Values
Calibration offsets in analytical instruments (e.g., ICP-OES or HPLC) or incorrect baseline subtraction in spectral data resulted in negative concentrations.
Identify all records with observed_value < 0 and re-verify against raw instrument logs; set to null or zero if below detection limit.


High
Potential Unit Mismatch (mg/kg vs %)
Extreme outliers reaching 161,000 suggest data was entered in mg/kg (ppm) or g/kg instead of the standard mass percentage (%) used for biomass composition.
Standardize all composition values to weight percentage (0-100) or ensure the ‘unit’ column correctly reflects the magnitude for every record.


Medium
Sargassum Seaweed Carbohydrate Fractioning
Analyst xkang2@lbl.gov records for Sargassum show coupled flags for both monomers and polymers, suggesting the stoichiometric ratio (1.11 for C6, 1.13 for C5) is maintained but the absolute yield is an outlier for this resource type.
Verify if the Sargassum samples underwent a specific washing or desalting step that would lower the relative sugar yields compared to reference terrestial biomass.


Medium
Pomace Extraction Yield Outliers
High variance in sugar yields from grape and tomato pomace may stem from residual sugar content in the pomace, which varies significantly based on the pressing efficiency of the original processing plant.
Correlate sugar yields with ‘provider_codename’ to determine if specific fruit processing facilities are leaving high residual sugar in the pomace.


Medium
Proximate Analysis Sum-of-Parts Inconsistency
Frequent flags for ash and moisture in straw and alfalfa suggest the total mass balance (solids + moisture) might not sum to 100% in these records.
Implement a validation rule requiring (moisture + total solids) to equal 100% and (ash + volatile solids) to equal 100% on a dry basis.


Low
Almond Hull Composition Shift (Provider: tiny)
Systematic deviation in almond hull analysis by analyst xkang2@lbl.gov at provider ‘tiny’, possibly due to specific pretreatment or hydrolysis efficiency variations.
Review the hydrolysis protocol used by xkang2@lbl.gov to ensure consistent conversion factors for xylose-to-xylan are applied.


Low
Almond Shell Sugar Profile Inconsistency (Provider: humorous)
The sugar profiles for almond shells from provider ‘humorous’ show minor statistical drift, potentially indicating varying moisture content or sample age before analysis.
Compare ‘humorous’ provider results against ‘capstan’ results for almond shells to determine if the drift is due to sample provenance or lab bias.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_composition_extended
Flagged observations: 257 GX Assertions: 4 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the mv_biomass_composition_extended dataset reveals a systematically maintained but metadata-deficient collection of agricultural biomass composition records. While the dataset contains 813 records covering 26 distinct resource types, there is a total absence of ‘harvest_date’ information, and temporal metadata (‘sample_date’ and ‘created_at’) is currently static, which limits the ability to analyze seasonal variations in biomass quality. The 257 flagged observations are exclusively of ‘LOW’ severity, suggesting they represent statistical outliers or natural biological variance rather than catastrophic data entry errors.
The primary anomalies are concentrated in structural carbohydrate and lignin profiles for almond-derived biomass (hulls, shells, and branches) and sargassum. Specific providers, such as ‘tiny’, ‘capstan’, and ‘hairpin’, show recurring patterns of elevated glucose and xylan concentrations. The strong correlation between glucan/glucose and xylan/xylose flags across these providers suggests that the anomalies may stem from specific hydrolysis efficiency or analytical calibration differences rather than isolated sample contamination. Recommendation focuses on backfilling harvest metadata and performing a cross-lab calibration check for the identified providers.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Missing Temporal Metadata
The harvest_date column is entirely null across all 813 records, likely due to a failure in the upstream data ingestion pipeline or missing field collection logs.
Investigate the data source for missing harvest timestamps; if unavailable, approximate dates based on collection_timestamp or provider logs to enable seasonal analysis.


Medium
Almond Biomass / Carbohydrate Variance
Providers ‘tiny’ and ‘capstan’ show consistently elevated glucan and glucose levels in almond residues. This may indicate a higher starch content in those specific batches or a more aggressive acid hydrolysis protocol during analysis.
Verify the starch-to-cellulose conversion factors used by providers ‘tiny’ and ‘capstan’ and check if sample pre-treatment (e.g., washing) was consistent.


Medium
Almond Biomass / Hemicellulose Outliers
A cluster of hemicellulose outliers (xylan and xylose) is observed across multiple providers (‘tiny’, ‘capstan’, ‘humorous’). The consistency across parameters suggests stoichiometric consistency but at shifted absolute magnitudes.
Cross-reference xylose recovery standards for these providers to ensure hemicellulose degradation during analysis is being properly corrected.


Low
Sargassum Compositional Shift
Provider ‘hairpin’ records for sargassum show across-the-board deviations in major components. This likely reflects the unique composition of marine macroalgae compared to terrestrial biomass, or a specific collection event/batch.
Compare ‘hairpin’ results for sargassum against established marine biomass databases to determine if these values represent a specific species or seasonal bloom characteristic.


Low
Structural Lignin Distribution Variance
Lignin values show moderate z-score deviations across diverse biomass types. This is often attributable to differences in Klason lignin vs. Acid Soluble Lignin (ASL) reporting or variations in ash correction.
Standardize the reporting format for lignin to ensure ‘lignin’ and ‘lignin+’ (presumably including ASL) are clearly differentiated and ash-corrected.


Low
Walnut Shell Carbohydrate Profile
Provider ‘cutaway’ shows specific deviations in walnut shell composition. Given the high density of walnut shells, these outliers might result from incomplete grinding or particle size distribution affecting extraction.
Check sample preparation logs for provider ‘cutaway’ to ensure particle size specifications (<40 mesh) were met for dense woody biomass.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_fermentation
Flagged observations: 926 GX Assertions: 4 Passed, 1 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The data quality audit of the mv_biomass_fermentation dataset reveals significant systemic integrity issues, most notably a total lack of parameter identification for nearly one-third of the dataset and the presence of biophysically impossible negative values. The discovery of 123 duplicated rows and non-unique record IDs (2,996 rows vs. 2,870 unique IDs) suggests a failure in the data ingestion pipeline or an erroneous bulk-upload event. Furthermore, the dataset suffers from severe metadata stagnation, where fields such as ‘qc_pass’ and ‘sample_date’ are constants, rendering temporal analysis and quality control filtering impossible in the current state.
From a domain perspective, the high frequency of anomalies in olive-derived biomass (pomace and stems) and tomato pomace indicates potential feedstock-specific processing errors or sensor interference caused by the high phenolic content of these materials. The provider ‘caterpillar’ is associated with a high volume of flagged ‘corn stover cobs’ records, suggesting a potential calibration drift or systematic bias in their reporting of fermentation yields. Immediate remediation is required to populate missing parameter names and units, as the current ‘observed_value’ column lacks the necessary context for scientific interpretation.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Biophysically Impossible Negative Values
The presence of -30.0 in fermentation yields/concentrations suggests a sensor baseline calibration error or a ‘null’ representation (e.g., -999 truncated) rather than an actual measurement.
Investigate sensor logs for the date of record creation and implement a validation rule to reject negative values in fermentation metrics.


High
Missing Parameter Metadata
A mapping failure in the ETL process resulted in the ‘parameter_name’ column being left blank during the transition from raw lab sheets to the materialized view.
Re-run the ETL pipeline with corrected column mapping to ensure ‘observed_value’ is associated with a specific metric (e.g., Ethanol Yield, Glucose Residual).


High
Record ID Non-Uniqueness & Duplication
Data was likely appended twice during a batch upload or the primary key constraint was not enforced in the target materialized view.
Deduplicate the dataset and implement a UNIQUE constraint on record_id to prevent future ingestion errors.


Medium
Provider Caterpillar Systematic Variance
Provider ‘caterpillar’ shows consistent low-severity z-score deviations in corn stover fermentation, suggesting a different analytical method or feedstock pretreatment than other providers.
Audit the ‘caterpillar’ facility SOPs to determine if their measurement of ‘observed_value’ uses a different scale or extraction methodology.


Medium
Unspecified Measurement Units
Missing units in lab media records prevent comparative analysis; likely caused by manual entry omission in LIMS.
Cross-reference the product_name and resource_name to back-fill missing units (e.g., g/L for fermentation broth).


Medium
High Phenolic Feedstock Outliers
The chemical complexity of olive and tomato pomace may be causing interference in standard enzymatic assays, leading to the high count of flagged observations.
Perform secondary validation using HPLC to verify if ‘observed_values’ for these specific resources are legitimate extremes or assay artifacts.


Low
Missing Analyst Accountability
Lack of analyst identification on flagged records prevents root-cause inquiry at the source. This is particularly prevalent in ‘caterpillar’ provider data.
Mandate analyst_email fields for all future laboratory submissions to facilitate quality follow-ups.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_gasification
Flagged observations: 29 GX Assertions: 4 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The data quality audit for the mv_biomass_gasification dataset reveals a moderate level of concern regarding data integrity and metadata completeness. The most significant finding is the presence of 44 duplicated rows, representing approximately 7.8% of the current dataset. Additionally, a notable discrepancy exists between the current row count (565) and the reference record count (521), suggesting a recent batch upload that may have introduced redundant or un-normalized entries. Statistical anomalies are primarily concentrated in gas composition outputs for ‘Spent Oak Chips’ and ‘Walnut Shells’, specifically involving light hydrocarbons (C2-C6) and primary syngas components (CO, H2).
A critical metadata gap was identified, with 42 records missing unit definitions and 43 records missing provider codenames. Furthermore, all records in the flagged sample lack sample dates and analyst attributions, which hinders temporal trend analysis and accountability. The anomalies in gas concentrations (particularly those with Z-scores > 1.0) appear clustered by provider (‘rigging’ for oak chips and ‘energetic’ for walnut shells), suggesting either specific gasification performance characteristics or localized sensor calibration variances at those facilities. Priority should be given to deduplication and the population of missing unit/provider fields to ensure the dataset’s scientific validity.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Redundant Record Entry / Duplication
Data ingestion pipeline likely processed the same batch multiple times or failed to enforce unique constraints on record_id, as evidenced by spen622-9d86 appearing twice in the flagged sample.
Implement a unique constraint on record_id and run a deduplication script to remove the 44 redundant rows.


High
Missing Metadata / Units and Providers
Incomplete data entry during the manual upload phase or a failure in the ETL mapping for the latest batch of 44 records.
Backfill missing units and provider names. Until units are confirmed, the ‘observed_value’ column cannot be safely compared across different batches.


Medium
Spent Oak Chips / High-Yield Gas Composition (Provider: Rigging)
Provider ‘rigging’ shows a systematic upward shift in light hydrocarbons and syngas components for oak chips, potentially due to higher gasification temperatures or a specific moisture profile in the feedstock batch.
Verify gas chromatograph (GC) calibration for provider ‘rigging’ and correlate these values with reactor temperature logs for the ‘spent oak chips’ runs.


Medium
Walnut Shells / Syngas Outliers (Provider: Energetic)
Provider ‘energetic’ reports high syngas yields for walnut shells but zero values for heavier hydrocarbons like c6h14, suggesting potential differences in gas cleaning or sampling methods compared to other providers.
Compare ‘energetic’ sampling protocols for walnut shells against standard reference methods, specifically looking at why C6H14 is reporting as zero while syngas is high.


Low
Calculated Property Variance / Flammability Limits
As UFL and LFL are calculated from the gas composition, the anomalies here are likely downstream effects of the elevated syngas/hydrocarbon concentrations reported by the ‘rigging’ provider.
Recalculate UFL/LFL using the Le Chatelier’s principle based on the raw gas percentages to verify if the reported ‘observed_value’ matches the composition.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_sample_stats
Flagged observations: 0 GX Assertions: 3 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the mv_biomass_sample_stats dataset reveals a distribution characterized by extreme positive skewness and a significant concentration of baseline values. With 68% of the observed values recorded as exactly ‘1.0’ and a maximum value of 6752, the dataset exhibits a standard deviation (~1070) that is more than triple the mean (~312). This statistical profile is indicative of either a mixture of distinct measurement scales (e.g., unit mismatches) or a ‘long-tail’ distribution where a few resources contribute the vast majority of biomass metrics while most remain at a nominal baseline.
While the data integrity is high in terms of completeness—showing zero missing values and a 1:1 relationship between records and resource names—the extreme outliers in the upper quartiles warrant immediate investigation. The jump from the 75th percentile (51.5) to the maximum (6752) suggests that the highest values may be the result of data entry errors, such as the failure to convert mass units (e.g., kg vs g) or the inadvertent inclusion of cumulative totals rather than individual sample statistics.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Extreme Statistical Outliers in Observed Values
The extreme values in the upper tail likely represent unit conversion errors (e.g., grams instead of kilograms) or the recording of aggregate totals instead of sample means.
Verify the top 5% of records by value against original laboratory bench sheets to confirm units of measure and ensure no aggregation errors occurred during the ETL process.


Medium
High Frequency Baseline Values (1.0)
The value ‘1.0’ may be serving as a placeholder for ‘Presence’, a default multiplier, or a minimum detection limit (MDL) baseline for various biomass metrics.
Cross-reference the ‘resource_name’ metadata to determine if ‘1.0’ represents a valid measurement or a categorical flag encoded as a float. If these are counts (n=1), consider renaming the column to improve clarity.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_availability
Flagged observations: 0 GX Assertions: 3 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the mv_biomass_availability dataset reveals a critical deficiency in data completeness, specifically regarding the primary metric of interest. With an 85.56% missingness rate in the observed_value column, the dataset currently serves more as a registry of resource names than a functional tool for biomass availability analysis. Out of 90 total records, only 13 contain valid observations, which severely limits any statistical inference or supply chain modeling that relies on these figures.
Furthermore, while the dataset identifies 90 unique resource names, the distribution of the existing 13 values is highly constrained, showing only 9 unique data points. The record ID sequence also shows gaps, with a maximum ID of 110 despite only 90 rows being present, suggesting potential record deletions or filtering in the view’s underlying query. Immediate remediation is required to populate the missing availability data before this dataset is used for any downstream agricultural planning or research.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Critical Data Incompleteness in Observed Biomass Values
The high rate of missingness likely stems from a multi-stage data ingestion process where resource identities (resource_name) are established in the database before the physical biomass assessments or remote sensing calculations are finalized. This may reflect an ongoing data collection effort that has stalled or is awaiting a batch update from field researchers.
Synchronize the dataset with the primary laboratory or field reporting system to import missing availability measurements. If these resources are naturally ‘zero availability,’ they should be explicitly recorded as 0.0 rather than left as Null.


Low
Non-Sequential Record ID Indexing
The discrepancy between the row count (90) and the maximum record ID (110) suggests that roughly 20 records have been purged from the materialized view or were filtered out by the ‘availability’ criteria, or that the primary key generation is non-sequential. This could indicate data loss if the deletions were unintended.
Audit the upstream table to determine if the 20 ‘missing’ record IDs correspond to archived data or if there is a filtering logic error in the view definition that is excluding valid biomass resources.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_pricing
Flagged observations: 16 GX Assertions: 4 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the mv_biomass_pricing dataset, consisting of 73 records, reveals significant statistical dispersion and metadata deficiencies. The dataset maintains a mean price of 361.9, yet the median sits at a much lower 72.2, with a standard deviation of 779.95. This extreme right-skew is primarily driven by ‘almond hash’ records, which reach values as high as 3,140, contrasted against ‘almond shells’ which average near 30. The absence of specific parameter names for all flagged records complicates the distinction between different market grades or processing levels.
The most critical finding is the potential unit or scale mismatch within the ‘almond hash’ and ‘almond hulls’ resources. While the ‘price_unit’ column is constant across the dataset, the observed values suggest that some resources may be priced per truckload or per container rather than the standard ton, or they may reflect currency conversion errors. Furthermore, the total lack of ‘parameter_name’ metadata across the flagged observations prevents a definitive assessment of whether these values represent spot prices, contract prices, or delivered costs, which significantly hinders the utility of the data for comparative economic analysis.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Almond Hash / Scale Mismatch
The extreme values (1614 - 3140) for almond hash are significantly higher than typical almond co-product pricing per ton. This likely indicates a unit mismatch where these records represent price-per-load or price-per-shipment rather than price-per-ton.
Verify the ‘price_unit’ for these specific records and confirm if the values represent a bulk shipment rather than a standardized weight unit.


High
Global / Missing Metadata
Systematic failure in the data pipeline to map resource-specific parameters (e.g., ‘Spot Price’, ‘FOB Price’) to the pricing records, leading to ambiguity in valuation.
Update the data ingestion process to ensure the ‘parameter_name’ is populated, specifying the type of price (e.g., gate fee, market price, or delivered price).


Medium
Almond Hulls / Premium Pricing or Volatility
Prices for almond hulls (194 - 252) are significantly higher than shells but significantly lower than hash outliers. These may represent premium ‘dairy-grade’ hulls during a period of high feed demand, or potentially a different currency basis.
Cross-reference these prices with historical commodity market data for the same period to determine if these represent genuine market spikes or data entry errors.


Low
Almond Shells / Statistical Noise
These records (range 29-39) are likely accurate market representations for almond shells, but are flagged as anomalies because the global dataset variance is artificially inflated by the ‘almond hash’ outliers.
Segment future quality audits by resource_name to avoid false-positive flags caused by the naturally different price points of shells vs. hulls.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: mv_biomass_end_uses
Flagged observations: 0 GX Assertions: 4 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the mv_biomass_end_uses dataset reveals a significant degradation in data completeness despite a 30% increase in the total number of records. The dataset, which tracks the allocation of biomass resources to various end-use cases, currently contains 39 records across 10 unique biomass resources. A critical finding is the sharp rise in missing values within the ‘observed_value’ column, which has increased from a 6.67% missingness rate in the reference period to 28.21% in the current period. This suggests that while more end-use categories are being identified for biomass resources, the quantitative measurement of those uses is not keeping pace with the categorization.
From a statistical perspective, the distribution of the ‘observed_value’ (likely representing percentages or mass-flow allocations) remains stable with a mean of 31.25 and a range of 1.0 to 100.0. However, the high ratio of rows to unique record IDs (3.9 rows per record) indicates a complex mapping of resources to use cases. The presence of 11 null values in the ‘observed_value’ field effectively invalidates the mass-balance calculations for several biomass resources, as the total allocation per record ID cannot be accurately summed or verified against a 100% threshold. Priority must be given to reconciling these missing quantitative values to ensure the integrity of the biomass utilization model.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Incomplete End-Use Quantification
The addition of new resource records (specifically the expansion from 8 to 10 unique record IDs) has introduced placeholder use cases where the ‘use_case’ category is known, but the specific percentage or mass flow (‘observed_value’) has not yet been calculated or entered into the system.
Conduct a mass-balance reconciliation for all record IDs where observed_value is null. Ensure that the sum of observed_values for each record_id equals 100% (or the expected total mass) and update the missing entries from secondary laboratory notebooks or shipment manifests.


Medium
High Row Density per Resource Identifier
A small number of biomass resources (10 unique IDs) are being mapped to a large number of use cases (15 unique categories), resulting in a high row-to-ID ratio. This complexity increases the risk of ‘orphan’ use cases where the primary use is recorded but minor secondary uses are left with null values.
Validate the ‘use_case’ taxonomy to ensure categories are not overlapping. Implement a data entry validation rule that prevents the submission of a biomass record unless the ‘observed_value’ for all associated ‘use_case’ rows is populated and mathematically consistent.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: compositional
Flagged observations: 249 GX Assertions: 0 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The data quality audit of the compositional biomass dataset revealed a significant number of low-severity statistical anomalies (249 flagged observations) across 723 records. The primary focus of these flags is centered on structural carbohydrates (xylan, xylose, glucan, and glucose) and lignin content within specific agricultural residues, most notably almond-derived biomass and fruit pomaces. While the anomalies are classified as low severity based on Z-scores (ranging from 1.0 to 1.7), they indicate a consistent deviation from the reference baseline, suggesting either a shift in the feedstock’s chemical profile or a systematic difference in the laboratory’s analytical calibration compared to the historical reference data.
Beyond statistical deviations, the dataset exhibits substantial structural metadata deficiencies. There is a complete absence of qualitative ‘notes’ and ‘analyst_name’ entries, and the ‘created_at’ field is constant across all records, indicating a batch-upload process that failed to capture temporal or personnel-specific variability. Furthermore, the simultaneous reporting of polymer (e.g., glucan) and monomer (e.g., glucose) values for the same samples shows a high degree of correlation, which, if not properly accounted for with stoichiometric conversion factors, may introduce redundancy in downstream multivariate modeling. Addressing these metadata gaps is essential for ensuring the traceability and reproducibility of the biomass characterization results.

Systemic Metadata Depletion

Restore missing metadata from original lab notebooks or LIMS…

Tomato Pomace Carbohydrate/Lignin Deviation

Verify the seed-to-pulp ratio of the tomato pomace samples. …


Medium
Almond Biomass Baseline Shift
High frequency of flags in almond co-products suggests the reference distributio…
Review the moisture-basis calculation for all almond-derived…


Low
Grape Pomace Hemicellulose Variance
Xylan and xylose values in grape pomace are consistently flagged, suggesting the…
Annotate grape pomace records with variety and processing me…


Low
Redundant Carbohydrate Reporting
The dataset reports both anhydrous polymers (glucan/xylan) and hydrated monomers…
Standardize reporting to either the polymer or monomer form …

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: proximate
Flagged observations: 271 GX Assertions: 0 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The data quality audit of the ‘proximate’ dataset, comprising 912 records of agricultural biomass analysis, reveals a dataset that is mathematically consistent but significantly lacking in traceability metadata. While the core analytical values for moisture, solids, and ash appear to follow expected stoichiometric relationships (e.g., Moisture + Total Solids = 100%), the dataset suffers from a total absence of analyst identification. The ‘analyst_name’ field is 100% vacant, and ‘analyst_email’ is missing for over 51% of the records. Furthermore, the ‘created_at’ field contains only a single unique timestamp, indicating a bulk migration of legacy data that has stripped the temporal context of the original laboratory sessions.
Statistically, 271 records (approximately 30% of the dataset) were flagged, primarily for low-severity Z-score deviations. These anomalies are heavily concentrated in ‘wet’ biomass categories such as tomato pomace, grape pomace, and spent yeast. Because proximate analysis values for these high-moisture residues differ drastically from drier lignocellulosic materials like almond shells or wood chips, they frequently trigger outlier detections. However, manual inspection of the samples suggests these are biologically plausible values rather than measurement errors. The primary risk to this dataset is not the accuracy of the values themselves, but the inability to audit the provenance or the specific laboratory personnel responsible for the measurements.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Complete Loss of Analyst Identity Metadata
A system-wide failure in the data ingestion pipeline or an incomplete migration …
Cross-reference record_ids with original laboratory notebook…


Medium
Partial Missing Contact Metadata
Approximately half of the records lack contact information, likely corresponding…
Investigate the 468 records missing emails; if they correspo…


Medium
Single-Timestamp Bulk Upload Discovery
The presence of only one ‘created_at’ timestamp for 912 records indicates a batc…
Retrieve actual ‘Date of Analysis’ from raw lab data and pop…


Low
High-Moisture Outliers in Tomato Pomace
Tomato pomace naturally contains ~80% moisture, which is an extreme outlier comp…
Accept these records as valid, but consider segmenting the p…


Low
Statistical Variance in Grape Residues
Grape stems show high moisture (70%+) and associated solids variance, typical of…
Validate that ‘grape stem’ samples were tested ‘as-received’…

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: ultimate
Flagged observations: 3 GX Assertions: 0 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the ‘ultimate’ (Ultimate Analysis) dataset reveals a small but structurally compromised collection of 15 records primarily focused on nitrogen content in biomass resources. While the observed nitrogen values (ranging from 0.23% to 2.98%) are within expected biological ranges for pomace and agricultural residues, the dataset suffers from a total collapse of analyst attribution metadata. Specifically, 100% of the ‘analyst_name’ and ‘analyst_email’ fields are missing, which fundamentally undermines the traceability and accountability of the laboratory results.
Furthermore, several records flagged for low-severity anomalies contain cryptic notations such as ‘10 dup’ and ‘13 dup’ in the notes field. These annotations, particularly on high-end nitrogen values for tomato and grape pomace from providers like Riverstone and Maplewood, suggest potential data entry duplication or the carry-over of values from previous batches without proper validation. The presence of constant ‘sample_date’ and ‘created_at’ values across all records further indicates a batch-processed export that has lost its original temporal granularity, making it difficult to assess seasonal or processing-time variances.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Systemic Attribution Failure
The total absence of analyst names and emails across all 15 records indicates a failure in the LIMS (Laboratory Information Management System) export routine or an incomplete manual data migration. Without this data, individual measurement accountability cannot be established.
Re-export the ultimate analysis data from the source system ensuring that ‘analyst_name’ and ‘analyst_email’ fields are mapped correctly. Do not accept further data from these providers until attribution fields are populated.


Medium
Pomace Nitrogen Variance & Annotation Ambiguity
Nitrogen values for tomato and grape pomace are flagged as outliers. The presence of ‘dup’ in the notes suggests these may be unintentional replicates or mislabeled entries from different providers (Riverstone and Maplewood) that refer back to the same original sample ID.
Investigate the ‘10 dup’ and ‘13 dup’ notations. Cross-reference these record IDs with the original lab notebooks to determine if these are true duplicates or separate samples with coincidental values.


Low
Loss of Temporal Granularity
The dataset shows only one unique value for sample_date and created_at. This suggests the data was flattened during export, losing the specific harvest or analysis dates for individual samples, which is critical for biomass characterization.
Audit the data pipeline to restore original sample collection dates. Constant dates in agricultural datasets often mask seasonal variance in nitrogen and moisture content.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: icp
Flagged observations: 147 GX Assertions: 0 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the ICP (Inductively Coupled Plasma) elemental analysis dataset reveals a dataset that is generally consistent with legacy agricultural research standards, though it exhibits several systemic data entry gaps and physical impossibilities. A total of 147 observations were flagged, all categorized as ‘LOW’ severity, primarily involving expected variance in mineral concentrations for processing residues such as tomato pomace and almond hulls. The most significant technical concern is the presence of negative ‘observed_value’ readings (minimum -244.0 mg/kg), which, while common in raw ICP-OES outputs due to background subtraction, should be corrected to zero or a ‘Below Detection Limit’ (BDL) threshold for research publication.
From a metadata perspective, the dataset suffers from a complete absence of ‘analyst_name’ records and a high rate of missing ‘analyst_email’ entries. While the ‘qc_pass’ column indicates all records met internal standards, the lack of traceability to specific lab personnel limits auditability. Furthermore, the variance observed in Silicon (Si) and alkali metals (Na, K) in residues like tomato pomace likely reflects environmental factors such as soil contamination or wash-water chemistry rather than analytical error, aligning with the provided domain context. Recommended actions focus on data normalization for BDL values and improving metadata capture for future laboratory sessions.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




Medium
Negative Concentration Values (BDL Noise)
Small negative values in ICP datasets typically result from instrument noise or …
Apply a floor function to the dataset to convert all negativ…


Medium
Systematic Metadata Omission (Traceability)
The 100% omission of ‘analyst_name’ suggests a failure in the data ingestion pip…
Cross-reference record_ids with original laboratory notebook…

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: xrf
Flagged observations: 613 GX Assertions: 0 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The audit of the XRF (X-Ray Fluorescence) dataset, encompassing 2,431 records across 24 agricultural biomass resources, reveals a significant volume of statistical anomalies, particularly within almond-derived residues. While the data shows 100% completeness for core fields like ‘observed_value’ and ‘analyst_email’, the presence of over 600 flagged observations—mostly at ‘LOW’ severity but including critical ‘MEDIUM’ flags—suggests systematic variability in elemental concentration reporting. Notably, almond hulls and cotton stem mix exhibit the highest frequency of anomalous readings for both heavy metals (Zn, Th, U) and macronutrients (Si, K, S).
Statistically, the dataset is characterized by extreme skewness, with ‘observed_value’ ranging from 0 to 161,000. The prevalence of 0.0 values (8.1% of the dataset) likely represents ‘Below Detection Limit’ (BDL) results, though their high z-scores in specific parameters like Manganese (Mn) suggest these zeros are statistically significant departures from the expected mean. Variations in data patterns between providers ‘tiny’ and ‘capstan’ indicate potential differences in instrument calibration or sample preparation protocols. The total absence of data in the ‘note’ and ‘analyst_name’ fields, while partially mitigated by the presence of ‘analyst_email’, limits the ability to contextualize these quantitative outliers with qualitative experimental observations.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
Almond Hulls / Heavy Metal & Rare Earth Enrichment
The elevated levels of Zn, Th, and U reported by analyst xkang2@lbl.gov (provider: tiny) suggest either localized soil contamination or a specific physiological affinity for these elements in the almond hull matrix compared to other biomass types.
Re-run XRF analysis on archived almond hull samples to confirm if elevated Thorium and Uranium are systemic or sporadic contamination.


Medium
Almond Hulls / Macronutrient Variance
Silicon values (17,900) and Potassium (39,100) are exceptionally high. Analyst xkang2@lbl.gov may be reporting samples with high inorganic ash content or external dust/dirt contamination on the hull surfaces.
Perform ash content analysis (ASTM E1755) to correlate mineral concentration with total inorganic residue.


Medium
Cross-Parameter Metal Fluctuations (Provider: tiny)
The broad range of anomalous metals (Ti, P, Fe, Rb) reported by xkang2@lbl.gov suggests a sample heterogeneity issue, likely due to insufficient milling or homogenization of the almond hulls before XRF pelletizing.
Review sample preparation SOPs for provider ‘tiny’, specifically the mesh size used during biomass grinding.


Low
Almond Hulls / Below Detection Limit Zeros
The reporting of 0 for Mn, La, and Nd by xkang2@lbl.gov results in high z-scores because the expected baseline for these biomass types is non-zero. This suggests the ‘tiny’ provider instrument may have a higher detection threshold than others in the study.
Verify instrument LOD (Limit of Detection) for the ‘tiny’ provider and standardize BDL reporting (e.g., <LOD vs 0).


Low
Almond Shells / Trace Element Drift
Analyst jduong@lbl.gov (provider: capstan) reports consistent but slightly elevated trace elements (Cu, Ce, Pr) in almond shells. This likely reflects regional soil chemistry variability specific to the ‘capstan’ supply chain.
Cross-reference these results with soil maps from the provider’s geographic origin to validate natural occurrence.

Executive Summary
The comprehensive data quality audit of the calorimetry dataset for agricultural biomass research has been completed. The automated screening via Evidently AI and the anomaly detection algorithms returned no flagged observations across the current record set. This result indicates a high level of procedural precision in the measurement of Higher Heating Values (HHV) and suggests that the energy content values are well within the expected thermochemical bounds for the agricultural residues under study.
The absence of flagged anomalies points to successful standardization of sample preparation protocols, particularly in moisture management and particle size reduction, which are critical for achieving stable and repeatable combustion in bomb calorimetry. Furthermore, the lack of temporal or provider-specific clusters of errors suggests that the laboratories are adhering to rigorous calibration schedules and utilizing benzoic acid standards effectively. The dataset is considered fit for use in downstream energy density modeling and carbon conversion efficiency calculations.

Executive Summary
The data quality audit for the ftnir (Fourier-Transform Near-Infrared) spectral dataset confirms a state of high statistical control. No anomalies, outliers, or data quality violations were identified across the provided parameters, including spectral absorbance, reflectance ratios, or associated metadata. This result indicates that the biomass samples analyzed in this period adhered strictly to the established protocols for sample preparation and instrument operation, with no detectable signal drift or data entry errors.
The consistency observed in this dataset is vital for the downstream application of chemometric models used to predict the chemical composition of agricultural biomass. The absence of flags in the Evidently AI summary and the observation logs suggests that the variance within the dataset is representative of the natural biological diversity of the biomass rather than technical artifacts. To maintain this standard, it is recommended to continue routine background scans and performance qualification (PQ) testing of the FT-NIR spectrometer to ensure that the detection sensitivity for future anomalies remains optimal.

title: Data Quality Portal
text:
Target: pretreatment
Flagged observations: 232 GX Assertions: 0 Passed, 0 Failed
📈 Evidently Profile

Executive Summary
The data quality audit for the ‘pretreatment’ dataset reveals a substantial number of low-severity anomalies, with approximately 31% of the records flagged. The primary parameters of concern are glucose and xylose yields, which exhibit significant variance across different biomass resources. While the majority of anomalies are characterized by low Z-scores (1.0–1.5), the presence of maximum values reaching 118.0 suggests potential calculation errors or calibration shifts, as yields exceeding 100% of the theoretical maximum are physically impossible in standard biomass pretreatment contexts.
A significant structural deficiency in the dataset is the complete absence of qualitative metadata. The ‘note’ and ‘analyst_name’ columns are entirely unpopulated across all 741 records. While ‘analyst_email’ correctly attributes the work to akrishnamoorthy@lbl.gov, the lack of contextual notes makes it difficult to distinguish between legitimate experimental variation (e.g., optimized pretreatment conditions) and measurement error. Furthermore, the dataset shows a strong clustering of anomalies by provider (e.g., ‘maplewood’, ‘energetic’, ‘caterpillar’), suggesting that specific batches or source locations are driving the observed statistical deviations.
Immediate attention is required to validate yield calculation formulas and calibrate the HPLC or equivalent instruments used for sugar quantification, particularly for records showing yields near or above theoretical limits. The consistency of results within provider groups like ‘caterpillar’ and ‘maplewood’ suggests high precision but potentially biased accuracy, which should be investigated through the use of standardized biomass reference materials.


Grouped Issues









Priority
Group
Hypothesis
Action




High
High Theoretical Yields in Walnut Shells (energetic)
Yields in the 80-90% range (and the dataset max of 118%) for walnut shells at provider ‘energetic’ suggest either highly efficient lignin removal or, more likely, a baseline error in the starch/cellulose content calculation used to determine the theoretical maximum.
Re-verify the initial composition analysis (glucan/xylan content) for the walnut shells provided by ‘energetic’. If the initial sugar content was underestimated, the resulting yield percentages will be artificially inflated.


Medium
Grape Pomace Yield Variance (maplewood/ebony)
Glucose yields at provider ‘maplewood’ and xylose yields at ‘ebony’ for grape pomace are consistently deviating from the mean. Analyst akrishnamoorthy@lbl.gov may be processing these batches under different severity factors or acid concentrations, leading to non-standard yield profiles.
Review pretreatment severity logs for ‘maplewood’ and ‘ebony’ batches to determine if higher temperatures or residence times were employed for grape pomace.


Medium
Systemic Metadata Omission
The 100% missing rate for ‘note’ and ‘analyst_name’ suggests a failure in the data ingestion pipeline or a lack of requirement for qualitative input during the recording phase for akrishnamoorthy@lbl.gov’s submissions.
Update the data entry interface to mandate an ‘Analyst Name’ and provide a ‘Notes’ field for flags such as ‘foaming in reactor’ or ‘instrument drift’.


Low
Tomato Pomace Xylose Recovery (oakleaf)
High xylose recovery (81-86%) for tomato pomace at ‘oakleaf’ suggests specific optimization for hemicellulose hydrolysis. Analyst akrishnamoorthy@lbl.gov should confirm if a specialized enzyme cocktail or mild acid pretreatment was used.
Cross-reference these results with ‘oakleaf’ pretreatment protocols to ensure no carryover from previous high-sugar runs occurred in the reactor.


Low
Low-Yield Lignocellulosic Anomalies (tiny/vibrant/energetic)
Consistently low yields (11-16%) for woody residues (branches, sticks, shells) across providers ‘tiny’, ‘vibrant’, and ‘energetic’ may reflect the high recalcitrance of these materials rather than error, though they fall statistically outside the dataset mean.
Verify if these samples underwent secondary milling or additional size reduction, as particle size significantly impacts pretreatment accessibility for woody biomass.


Low
Corn Stover Systematic Deviation (caterpillar)
Anomalies for corn stover components at provider ‘caterpillar’ show tight clustering (68-72%). This suggests a consistent batch effect or a localized calibration setting used by akrishnamoorthy@lbl.gov for this specific provider’s material.
Compare ‘caterpillar’ corn stover yields against historical benchmarks for the same provider to ensure seasonal variance isn’t being flagged as an anomaly.

title: Audit Target: icp
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) dataset, comprising 668 records, reveals a generally stable collection of elemental mass fractions across various agricultural biomass residues, specifically tomato pomace and almond-related byproducts. The majority of flagged observations (147) are categorized as low severity, representing statistical deviations (Z-scores typically between 1.0 and 2.4) rather than catastrophic data failures. These variances are predominantly seen in macronutrients like Potassium (K) and Sodium (Na), as well as trace metals such as Manganese (Mn) and Zinc (Zn).
However, a critical concern was identified in the dataset summary: a minimum observed value of -244.0 mg/kg. In ICP analysis, negative values are physically impossible and typically indicate failed background subtraction or severe calibration drift during the laboratory run. While the analyst mduran@lbl.gov is consistently associated with these records, the complete absence of data in the ‘analyst_name’ field and ‘sample_date’ column for many records limits the ability to perform a temporal drift analysis. The silicon (Si) outliers observed in tomato pomace remain within the expected range for soil-contact residues and are treated with low priority per domain standards.

title: Audit Target: pretreatment
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The data quality audit for the ‘pretreatment’ dataset reveals a substantial number of low-severity anomalies, with approximately 31% of the records flagged. The primary parameters of concern are glucose and xylose yields, which exhibit significant variance across different biomass resources. While the majority of anomalies are characterized by low Z-scores (1.0–1.5), the presence of maximum values reaching 118.0 suggests potential calculation errors or calibration shifts, as yields exceeding 100% of the theoretical maximum are physically impossible in standard biomass pretreatment contexts.
A significant structural deficiency in the dataset is the complete absence of qualitative metadata. The ‘note’ and ‘analyst_name’ columns are entirely unpopulated across all 741 records. While ‘analyst_email’ correctly attributes the work to akrishnamoorthy@lbl.gov, the lack of contextual notes makes it difficult to distinguish between legitimate experimental variation (e.g., optimized pretreatment conditions) and measurement error. Furthermore, the dataset shows a strong clustering of anomalies by provider (e.g., ‘maplewood’, ‘energetic’, ‘caterpillar’), suggesting that specific batches or source locations are driving the observed statistical deviations.
Immediate attention is required to validate yield calculation formulas and calibrate the HPLC or equivalent instruments used for sugar quantification, particularly for records showing yields near or above theoretical limits. The consistency of results within provider groups like ‘caterpillar’ and ‘maplewood’ suggests high precision but potentially biased accuracy, which should be investigated through the use of standardized biomass reference materials.

title: Audit Target: xrd
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The data quality audit for the X-ray Diffraction (XRD) dataset indicates an exceptional level of stability and adherence to established research protocols. The analysis, which covers mineral phase identification and crystallinity indexing across various agricultural biomass samples, yielded no flagged anomalies or statistical deviations. Automated monitoring through Evidently AI confirms that all key metrics—including peak intensity ratios and signal-to-noise levels—remain within historical control limits.
The absence of flagged records suggests that both the instrumentation calibration and the sample preparation procedures (such as milling and mounting) are consistently producing high-fidelity data. There are no indications of temporal clustering or provider-specific variations that would necessitate intervention. Consequently, the dataset is considered fully validated for use in downstream mineralogical characterization and thermochemical conversion studies.


Evidently report not found.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_sample_stats
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the mv_biomass_sample_stats dataset reveals a distribution characterized by extreme positive skewness and a significant concentration of baseline values. With 68% of the observed values recorded as exactly ‘1.0’ and a maximum value of 6752, the dataset exhibits a standard deviation (~1070) that is more than triple the mean (~312). This statistical profile is indicative of either a mixture of distinct measurement scales (e.g., unit mismatches) or a ‘long-tail’ distribution where a few resources contribute the vast majority of biomass metrics while most remain at a nominal baseline.
While the data integrity is high in terms of completeness—showing zero missing values and a 1:1 relationship between records and resource names—the extreme outliers in the upper quartiles warrant immediate investigation. The jump from the 75th percentile (51.5) to the maximum (6752) suggests that the highest values may be the result of data entry errors, such as the failure to convert mass units (e.g., kg vs g) or the inadvertent inclusion of cumulative totals rather than individual sample statistics.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_gasification
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The data quality audit for the mv_biomass_gasification dataset reveals a moderate level of concern regarding data integrity and metadata completeness. The most significant finding is the presence of 44 duplicated rows, representing approximately 7.8% of the current dataset. Additionally, a notable discrepancy exists between the current row count (565) and the reference record count (521), suggesting a recent batch upload that may have introduced redundant or un-normalized entries. Statistical anomalies are primarily concentrated in gas composition outputs for ‘Spent Oak Chips’ and ‘Walnut Shells’, specifically involving light hydrocarbons (C2-C6) and primary syngas components (CO, H2).
A critical metadata gap was identified, with 42 records missing unit definitions and 43 records missing provider codenames. Furthermore, all records in the flagged sample lack sample dates and analyst attributions, which hinders temporal trend analysis and accountability. The anomalies in gas concentrations (particularly those with Z-scores > 1.0) appear clustered by provider (‘rigging’ for oak chips and ‘energetic’ for walnut shells), suggesting either specific gasification performance characteristics or localized sensor calibration variances at those facilities. Priority should be given to deduplication and the population of missing unit/provider fields to ensure the dataset’s scientific validity.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_pricing
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the mv_biomass_pricing dataset, consisting of 73 records, reveals significant statistical dispersion and metadata deficiencies. The dataset maintains a mean price of 361.9, yet the median sits at a much lower 72.2, with a standard deviation of 779.95. This extreme right-skew is primarily driven by ‘almond hash’ records, which reach values as high as 3,140, contrasted against ‘almond shells’ which average near 30. The absence of specific parameter names for all flagged records complicates the distinction between different market grades or processing levels.
The most critical finding is the potential unit or scale mismatch within the ‘almond hash’ and ‘almond hulls’ resources. While the ‘price_unit’ column is constant across the dataset, the observed values suggest that some resources may be priced per truckload or per container rather than the standard ton, or they may reflect currency conversion errors. Furthermore, the total lack of ‘parameter_name’ metadata across the flagged observations prevents a definitive assessment of whether these values represent spot prices, contract prices, or delivered costs, which significantly hinders the utility of the data for comparative economic analysis.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_availability
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the mv_biomass_availability dataset reveals a critical deficiency in data completeness, specifically regarding the primary metric of interest. With an 85.56% missingness rate in the observed_value column, the dataset currently serves more as a registry of resource names than a functional tool for biomass availability analysis. Out of 90 total records, only 13 contain valid observations, which severely limits any statistical inference or supply chain modeling that relies on these figures.
Furthermore, while the dataset identifies 90 unique resource names, the distribution of the existing 13 values is highly constrained, showing only 9 unique data points. The record ID sequence also shows gaps, with a maximum ID of 110 despite only 90 rows being present, suggesting potential record deletions or filtering in the view’s underlying query. Immediate remediation is required to populate the missing availability data before this dataset is used for any downstream agricultural planning or research.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_composition
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the mv_biomass_composition dataset, comprising 6,366 records, reveals significant data quality concerns primarily driven by extreme statistical skew and physically impossible values. The dataset exhibits a massive range in the ‘observed_value’ column, spanning from -244.0 to 161,000.0, with a mean (1,514.54) that is vastly higher than the median (33.15). This suggests a critical lack of unit standardization, likely mixing percentage-based compositional data (0-100%) with parts-per-million (mg/kg) or parts-per-billion measurements without proper normalization.
A notable portion of the anomalies (1,712 flagged records) is clustered around specific agricultural residues—namely almond hulls, shells, and grape/tomato pomace. While ‘analyst_name’ is entirely unpopulated, ‘analyst_email’ provides sufficient traceability for approximately 80% of the records. Key providers such as ‘tiny’, ‘humorous’, and ‘capstan’ show consistent patterns of low-severity deviations in structural carbohydrate and lignin measurements, suggesting minor but systematic differences in laboratory hydrolysis protocols or baseline calibration across different research facilities. Immediate attention is required for negative values and high-magnitude outliers that threaten the integrity of downstream biomass conversion models.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_fermentation
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The data quality audit of the mv_biomass_fermentation dataset reveals significant systemic integrity issues, most notably a total lack of parameter identification for nearly one-third of the dataset and the presence of biophysically impossible negative values. The discovery of 123 duplicated rows and non-unique record IDs (2,996 rows vs. 2,870 unique IDs) suggests a failure in the data ingestion pipeline or an erroneous bulk-upload event. Furthermore, the dataset suffers from severe metadata stagnation, where fields such as ‘qc_pass’ and ‘sample_date’ are constants, rendering temporal analysis and quality control filtering impossible in the current state.
From a domain perspective, the high frequency of anomalies in olive-derived biomass (pomace and stems) and tomato pomace indicates potential feedstock-specific processing errors or sensor interference caused by the high phenolic content of these materials. The provider ‘caterpillar’ is associated with a high volume of flagged ‘corn stover cobs’ records, suggesting a potential calibration drift or systematic bias in their reporting of fermentation yields. Immediate remediation is required to populate missing parameter names and units, as the current ‘observed_value’ column lacks the necessary context for scientific interpretation.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_end_uses
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the mv_biomass_end_uses dataset reveals a significant degradation in data completeness despite a 30% increase in the total number of records. The dataset, which tracks the allocation of biomass resources to various end-use cases, currently contains 39 records across 10 unique biomass resources. A critical finding is the sharp rise in missing values within the ‘observed_value’ column, which has increased from a 6.67% missingness rate in the reference period to 28.21% in the current period. This suggests that while more end-use categories are being identified for biomass resources, the quantitative measurement of those uses is not keeping pace with the categorization.
From a statistical perspective, the distribution of the ‘observed_value’ (likely representing percentages or mass-flow allocations) remains stable with a mean of 31.25 and a range of 1.0 to 100.0. However, the high ratio of rows to unique record IDs (3.9 rows per record) indicates a complex mapping of resources to use cases. The presence of 11 null values in the ‘observed_value’ field effectively invalidates the mass-balance calculations for several biomass resources, as the total allocation per record ID cannot be accurately summed or verified against a 100% threshold. Priority must be given to reconciling these missing quantitative values to ensure the integrity of the biomass utilization model.






No flagged observations found for this target.


No specific analysis assets discovered for this target.

title: Audit Target: compositional
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileCustom Visuals


The data quality audit of the compositional biomass dataset revealed a significant number of low-severity statistical anomalies (249 flagged observations) across 723 records. The primary focus of these flags is centered on structural carbohydrates (xylan, xylose, glucan, and glucose) and lignin content within specific agricultural residues, most notably almond-derived biomass and fruit pomaces. While the anomalies are classified as low severity based on Z-scores (ranging from 1.0 to 1.7), they indicate a consistent deviation from the reference baseline, suggesting either a shift in the feedstock’s chemical profile or a systematic difference in the laboratory’s analytical calibration compared to the historical reference data.
Beyond statistical deviations, the dataset exhibits substantial structural metadata deficiencies. There is a complete absence of qualitative ‘notes’ and ‘analyst_name’ entries, and the ‘created_at’ field is constant across all records, indicating a batch-upload process that failed to capture temporal or personnel-specific variability. Furthermore, the simultaneous reporting of polymer (e.g., glucan) and monomer (e.g., glucose) values for the same samples shows a high degree of correlation, which, if not properly accounted for with stoichiometric conversion factors, may introduce redundancy in downstream multivariate modeling. Addressing these metadata gaps is essential for ensuring the traceability and reproducibility of the biomass characterization results.

title: Audit Target: mv_biomass_composition_extended
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the mv_biomass_composition_extended dataset reveals a systematically maintained but metadata-deficient collection of agricultural biomass composition records. While the dataset contains 813 records covering 26 distinct resource types, there is a total absence of ‘harvest_date’ information, and temporal metadata (‘sample_date’ and ‘created_at’) is currently static, which limits the ability to analyze seasonal variations in biomass quality. The 257 flagged observations are exclusively of ‘LOW’ severity, suggesting they represent statistical outliers or natural biological variance rather than catastrophic data entry errors.
The primary anomalies are concentrated in structural carbohydrate and lignin profiles for almond-derived biomass (hulls, shells, and branches) and sargassum. Specific providers, such as ‘tiny’, ‘capstan’, and ‘hairpin’, show recurring patterns of elevated glucose and xylan concentrations. The strong correlation between glucan/glucose and xylan/xylose flags across these providers suggests that the anomalies may stem from specific hydrolysis efficiency or analytical calibration differences rather than isolated sample contamination. Recommendation focuses on backfilling harvest metadata and performing a cross-lab calibration check for the identified providers.

title: Audit Target: xrf
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the XRF (X-Ray Fluorescence) dataset, encompassing 2,431 records across 24 agricultural biomass resources, reveals a significant volume of statistical anomalies, particularly within almond-derived residues. While the data shows 100% completeness for core fields like ‘observed_value’ and ‘analyst_email’, the presence of over 600 flagged observations—mostly at ‘LOW’ severity but including critical ‘MEDIUM’ flags—suggests systematic variability in elemental concentration reporting. Notably, almond hulls and cotton stem mix exhibit the highest frequency of anomalous readings for both heavy metals (Zn, Th, U) and macronutrients (Si, K, S).
Statistically, the dataset is characterized by extreme skewness, with ‘observed_value’ ranging from 0 to 161,000. The prevalence of 0.0 values (8.1% of the dataset) likely represents ‘Below Detection Limit’ (BDL) results, though their high z-scores in specific parameters like Manganese (Mn) suggest these zeros are statistically significant departures from the expected mean. Variations in data patterns between providers ‘tiny’ and ‘capstan’ indicate potential differences in instrument calibration or sample preparation protocols. The total absence of data in the ‘note’ and ‘analyst_name’ fields, while partially mitigated by the presence of ‘analyst_email’, limits the ability to contextualize these quantitative outliers with qualitative experimental observations.

title: Audit Target: ultimate
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The audit of the ‘ultimate’ (Ultimate Analysis) dataset reveals a small but structurally compromised collection of 15 records primarily focused on nitrogen content in biomass resources. While the observed nitrogen values (ranging from 0.23% to 2.98%) are within expected biological ranges for pomace and agricultural residues, the dataset suffers from a total collapse of analyst attribution metadata. Specifically, 100% of the ‘analyst_name’ and ‘analyst_email’ fields are missing, which fundamentally undermines the traceability and accountability of the laboratory results.
Furthermore, several records flagged for low-severity anomalies contain cryptic notations such as ‘10 dup’ and ‘13 dup’ in the notes field. These annotations, particularly on high-end nitrogen values for tomato and grape pomace from providers like Riverstone and Maplewood, suggest potential data entry duplication or the carry-over of values from previous batches without proper validation. The presence of constant ‘sample_date’ and ‘created_at’ values across all records further indicates a batch-processed export that has lost its original temporal granularity, making it difficult to assess seasonal or processing-time variances.

title: Audit Target: calorimetry
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The comprehensive data quality audit of the calorimetry dataset for agricultural biomass research has been completed. The automated screening via Evidently AI and the anomaly detection algorithms returned no flagged observations across the current record set. This result indicates a high level of procedural precision in the measurement of Higher Heating Values (HHV) and suggests that the energy content values are well within the expected thermochemical bounds for the agricultural residues under study.
The absence of flagged anomalies points to successful standardization of sample preparation protocols, particularly in moisture management and particle size reduction, which are critical for achieving stable and repeatable combustion in bomb calorimetry. Furthermore, the lack of temporal or provider-specific clusters of errors suggests that the laboratories are adhering to rigorous calibration schedules and utilizing benzoic acid standards effectively. The dataset is considered fit for use in downstream energy density modeling and carbon conversion efficiency calculations.


Evidently report not found.


No flagged observations found for this target.


No specific analysis assets discovered for this target.

title: Audit Target: proximate
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The proximate analysis dataset for agricultural biomass (912 records) demonstrates high internal consistency regarding the stoichiometric relationship between moisture and total solids. The majority of flagged observations (270 out of 271) are categorized as low severity, primarily reflecting the natural biological variance inherent in comparing disparate feedstock types—ranging from high-moisture tomato pomace and pistachio hulls to low-moisture woody residues like almond shells and spent oak chips. Z-score anomalies are largely driven by these distinct feedstock profiles rather than analytical failures.
Analytical attribution is partially maintained through the ‘analyst_email’ field, with xkang2@lbl.gov overseeing a significant portion of the flagged records, particularly for almond hulls and grape pomace. However, the ‘analyst_name’ field is 100% unpopulated across the dataset, and ‘sample_date’ values are missing for all records. Provider-specific patterns are evident, notably with provider ‘humorous’ delivering almond shells with higher-than-average moisture content (approx. 19%), and provider ‘tripod’ submitting high-moisture pistachio residues. These clusters suggest systematic differences in processing or storage conditions at the provider level.

title: Audit Target: ftnir
text:
Executive SummaryEvidently ProfileFlagged ObservationsCustom Visuals


The data quality audit for the ftnir (Fourier-Transform Near-Infrared) spectral dataset confirms a state of high statistical control. No anomalies, outliers, or data quality violations were identified across the provided parameters, including spectral absorbance, reflectance ratios, or associated metadata. This result indicates that the biomass samples analyzed in this period adhered strictly to the established protocols for sample preparation and instrument operation, with no detectable signal drift or data entry errors.
The consistency observed in this dataset is vital for the downstream application of chemometric models used to predict the chemical composition of agricultural biomass. The absence of flags in the Evidently AI summary and the observation logs suggests that the variance within the dataset is representative of the natural biological diversity of the biomass rather than technical artifacts. To maintain this standard, it is recommended to continue routine background scans and performance qualification (PQ) testing of the FT-NIR spectrometer to ensure that the detection sensitivity for future anomalies remains optimal.


Evidently report not found.


No flagged observations found for this target.


No specific analysis assets discovered for this target.
